examples.title: Banker's Safety Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode to check if system is in safe state
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Safety
INPUT: Available[1..m], Maximum[1..n][1..m], Allocation[1..n][1..m]
OUTPUT: SafeSequence[1..n], SafeState (TRUE/FALSE)

BEGIN
  // Calculate Need matrix
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    FOR j = 1 TO m DO
      Need[i][j] = Maximum[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]
    END FOR
  END FOR

  // Work = Available, Finish[i] = FALSE
  Work[1..m] = Available[1..m]
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    Finish[i] = FALSE
  END FOR

  // Find safe sequence
  WHILE count < n DO
    found = FALSE
    FOR i = 1 TO n DO
      IF Finish[i] = FALSE AND Need[i] ≤ Work THEN
        // Process can finish
        Work = Work + Allocation[i]
        Finish[i] = TRUE
        SafeSequence[count] = i
        count = count + 1
        found = TRUE
      END IF
    END FOR
    IF found = FALSE THEN
      // No safe process found
      RETURN FALSE
    END IF
  END WHILE

  RETURN TRUE
END
examples.explanation: The safety algorithm checks if the system can allocate resources such that all processes can complete without deadlock. It simulates resource allocation and checks if each process can finish with available resources plus what it already holds.

examples.title: Banker's Resource Request Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to handle resource request from a process
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Resource_Request
INPUT: Process P, Request[1..m]

BEGIN
  // Step 1: Check if request is valid
  IF Request > Need[P] THEN
    ERROR: Process exceeded maximum claim
    RETURN
  END IF

  // Step 2: Check if enough resources available
  IF Request > Available THEN
    // Process must wait
    RETURN
  END IF

  // Step 3: Pretend to allocate
  Available = Available - Request
  Allocation[P] = Allocation[P] + Request
  Need[P] = Need[P] - Request

  // Step 4: Check safety
  IF Safety() = TRUE THEN
    // Request granted
    RETURN
  ELSE
    // Rollback - request denied
    Available = Available + Request
    Allocation[P] = Allocation[P] - Request
    Need[P] = Need[P] + Request
    // Process must wait
    RETURN
  END IF
END
examples.explanation: When a process requests resources, the Banker's algorithm first validates the request, then temporarily allocates resources, runs the safety check, and only grants the request if the system remains in a safe state.

examples.title: Deadlock Detection Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to detect deadlock for multiple resource instances
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Deadlock_Detection
INPUT: Available[1..m], Allocation[1..n][1..m], Request[1..n][1..m]
OUTPUT: List of deadlocked processes

BEGIN
  // Work = Available
  Work[1..m] = Available[1..m]

  // Finish[i] = (Allocation[i] == 0)
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    IF Allocation[i] = AllZero THEN
      Finish[i] = TRUE
    ELSE
      Finish[i] = FALSE
    END IF
  END FOR

  // Find processes that can complete
  REPEAT
    found = FALSE
    FOR i = 1 TO n DO
      IF Finish[i] = FALSE AND Request[i] ≤ Work THEN
        // Process can complete
        Work = Work + Allocation[i]
        Finish[i] = TRUE
        found = TRUE
      END IF
    END FOR
  UNTIL found = FALSE

  // Check for deadlock
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    IF Finish[i] = FALSE THEN
      // Process i is deadlocked
      ADD i TO DeadlockList
    END IF
  END FOR

  RETURN DeadlockList
END
examples.explanation: This detection algorithm works by checking if processes with zero allocation can finish first, then iteratively checking if remaining processes can complete with available resources. Any unmarked process after the algorithm completes is deadlocked.

examples.title: Wait-for Graph Construction
examples.description: C code to construct wait-for graph from resource allocation graph
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates wait-for graph construction and deadlock detection. The wait-for graph is derived from the Resource Allocation Graph by creating edges from waiting processes to processes holding the required resources. A cycle in this graph indicates deadlock.
